bins.name: FERC transmission-only jurisdiction
bins.desc: DPN supports limiting FERC authority to direct transmission interconnections to preserve state regulatory control over generation and intrastate facilities. The filing cites NARUC opposition to federal override of state jurisdiction as evidence of widespread concern.
quotes: First, to avoid even arguably affecting the States' jurisdiction over generation facilities, facilities used in local distribution or only for the transmission of electric energy in intrastate commerce, or transmissions consumed by the transmitter, the Commission's jurisdiction should be limited to interconnections directly to transmission facilities, consistent with the Commission's seven-factor test.
The NARUC resolution signals that state regulators nationwide are opposed to the Department of Energy's effort to enable FERC to supplant states' authority over large load interconnections.

bins.name: 20 MW threshold with developer flexibility
bins.desc: DPN supports 20 MW as the baseline threshold but advocates for exceptions on smaller facilities to enable developer growth and expansion pathways. Exceptions should support start-up phases.
quotes: Second, consistent with the Commission's pro forma LGIP and LGIA, the reforms should only apply to new loads greater than 20 MW and, for hybrid facilities, where the load is greater than 20 MW. We seek comment on alternative thresholds, including whether such a threshold is necessary at all.
By still setting the threshold at 20 MW but allowing exceptions for certain smaller facilities, it will give developers the opportunity to start-up, operate, and then expand.

bins.name: Standardized study deposits to deter speculation
bins.desc: DPN supports standardized, non-trivial study deposits modeled on Texas SB 6 ($100k minimum) to prevent speculative flooding of interconnection requests. First-year credit-back ensures financial access for serious developers.
quotes: Fourth, like generating facilities, load and hybrid facilities should be subject to standardized study deposits, readiness requirements, and withdrawal penalties. These provisions deter speculative projects and provide transmission providers with more useful information to more accurately forecast demand on their systems.

Study deposits would assist in solving the problem of entities flooding utilities with requests for load. Utilities are not capable of deciphering real market participants or developers fishing projects to others.

The deposit needs to be non-trivial, somewhat of a deterrent but not excessive. For example, in 2025, Texas enacted SB 6 which includes standards that 'must set a flat study fee of at least $100,000 to be paid to the interconnecting electric utility or municipally owned utility for initial transmission screening studies for large loads' (§ 37.0561(f)). Given that data centers are not subject to paying these deposits now, these payments should be credited back over the first year of the contract.

bins.name: Non-firm loads excluded from deliverability study
bins.desc: DPN proposes excluding non-firm load classifications from deliverability studies since those studies only ensure firm connectivity, not flexible or curtailable load profiles.
quotes: However, if a new classification of non-firm loads and backup generation is to be established through this ruling, then these loads should be excluded from the deliverability study requirement as this studies intention is to ensure connection to the grid is firm.

bins.name: Flexible hybrid injection/withdrawal rights
bins.desc: DPN advocates flexible hybrid facilities studied on actual requested withdrawal and injection rights, with future revision capability as transmission capacity evolves. Non-firm withdrawal rights enable excess load monetization.
quotes: Fifth, hybrid facilities should be studied based on the amount of injection and / or withdrawal rights requested. For example, a hybrid facility consisting of a 500 MW load and a 600 MW generating facility may seek no withdrawal rights and 100MW of injection rights. This provides incentives for co-location with new generation facilities and ensures efficient buildout of the transmission system.
Hybrid facilities need to have the ability for future revisions to their injection and / or withdrawal rights as the transmission system capacity increases / improves.

Additionally, with this new classification of load types, FERC should also require that flexible loads have non-firm withdrawal rights, ensuring that excess load on the grid can be monetized during period of low demand if there is a demand from non-firm data centers to consume additional power.

bins.name: System protection for hybrid rights enforcement
bins.desc: DPN supports required system protection facilities to prevent unauthorized injections or withdrawals beyond agreed rights. Precedent exists in TVA load profile enforcement with penalty mechanisms.
quotes: Sixth, any hybrid interconnection shall be required to install the system protection facilities necessary to prevent unauthorized injections or withdrawals that exceed the respective rights.

Hybrid facilities are already required to control their load profile. Typically, any non-compliance will involve penalties. This is what occurs in TVA for loads that are supposed to be strictly off-peak but happen to operate during the peak hours — results in a stiff penalty charge.

bins.name: Expedited study process for curtailable loads
bins.desc: DPN strongly supports expedited interconnection study for loads that commit to curtailment and dispatchability, with higher priority for proven operators. Existing queue participants should have opt-in access to expedited track.
quotes: Seventh, the interconnection study of large loads that agree to be curtailable and hybrid facilities that agree to be curtailable and dispatchable should be expedited.
This reform is essential. There also needs to be an even higher priority for company submissions that have proven their ability to curtail / dispatched at their facilities vs. new entries.

For large loads already undergoing the study process, if their current process will take more than 60 days, they should be given the option to opt into the expedited interconnection study queue as first in line.
There must be a clear pathway to interconnect non-firm, flexible large load- SPP is already considering a process called PALS so examples exists.

bins.name: Network upgrade cost recovery over PPA term
bins.desc: DPN affirms loads fund 100% of assigned network upgrades but credits should be issued over the PPA term, with maximum credit duration set at 50% of PPA length for financial planning clarity.
quotes: Eighth, load and hybrid facilities should be responsible for 100% of the network upgrades that they are assigned through the interconnection studies.

This is already being done, but the upgrade costs need to be credited back to the facility over the course the power purchase agreement (PPA) length. The maximum duration for issuing the credit should be 50% of the PPA length.

bins.name: Joint load-generation study design
bins.desc: DPN advocates co-locating load and generation studies to minimize costly network upgrades through efficient siting. Current vacuum isolation of load studies is inefficient.
quotes: Third, to the extent practicable, load and hybrid facilities should be studied together with generating facilities. Such an approach will allow for efficient siting of loads and generating facilities and thereby minimize the need for costly network upgrades.
Load studies do not need to be reviewed in a vacuum like they are now.

Fourth, like generating facilities, load and hybrid facilities should be subject to standardized study deposits, readiness requirements, and withdrawal penalties. These provisions deter speculative projects and provide transmission providers with more useful information to more accurately forecast demand on their systems.

bins.name: Cost-efficient interconnection through co-location
bins.desc: DPN argues joint load-generation studies and co-location incentives reduce transmission development costs. Load design transparency aligns with utility cost-recovery mechanisms.
quotes: Third, to the extent practicable, load and hybrid facilities should be studied together with generating facilities. Such an approach will allow for efficient siting of loads and generating facilities and thereby minimize the need for costly network upgrades.
Eighth, load and hybrid facilities should be responsible for 100% of the network upgrades that they are assigned through the interconnection studies.

bins.name: Co-location incentives for loads and generation
bins.desc: DPN emphasizes hybrid facility incentives for co-locating near new generation to ensure efficient transmission system buildout. Rights-based design encourages proximate placement.
quotes: Fifth, hybrid facilities should be studied based on the amount of injection and / or withdrawal rights requested. For example, a hybrid facility consisting of a 500 MW load and a 600 MW generating facility may seek no withdrawal rights and 100MW of injection rights. This provides incentives for co-location with new generation facilities and ensures efficient buildout of the transmission system.

bins.name: SPP PALS precedent for non-firm interconnection
bins.desc: DPN references SPP's existing PALS process as a clear precedent for establishing non-firm flexible load pathways.
quotes: There must be a clear pathway to interconnect non-firm, flexible large load- SPP is already considering a process called PALS so examples exists.

bins.name: Flexible load integration priority
bins.desc: DPN frames expedited integration of flexible digital loads as critical to grid resilience as demand rises and queues constrain.
quotes: As large load demand rises and interconnection queues are constrained, FERC's support for expediting the integration of flexible loads is critical to bolster the nation's power systems.

bins.name: Interconnection queue congestion
bins.desc: DPN identifies constrained interconnection queues as a barrier to timely large load deployment.
quotes: As large load demand rises and interconnection queues are constrained, FERC's support for expediting the integration of flexible loads is critical to bolster the nation's power systems.

bins.name: Growth pathways for developers
bins.desc: DPN supports threshold exceptions to enable developer start-up, operational proof, and expansion phases.
quotes: By still setting the threshold at 20 MW but allowing exceptions for certain smaller facilities, it will give developers the opportunity to start-up, operate, and then expand.

bins.name: Load-generation co-siting analysis
bins.desc: DPN advocates abandoning vacuum analysis of load studies in favor of integrated load-generation siting assessment.
quotes: Load studies do not need to be reviewed in a vacuum like they are now.

bins.name: Texas SB 6 deposit standard
bins.desc: DPN cites Texas $100k deposit precedent as an appropriate scale for deterring speculation while remaining accessible.
quotes: The deposit needs to be non-trivial, somewhat of a deterrent but not excessive. For example, in 2025, Texas enacted SB 6 which includes standards that 'must set a flat study fee of at least $100,000 to be paid to the interconnecting electric utility or municipally owned utility for initial transmission screening studies for large loads' (§ 37.0561(f)). Given that data centers are not subject to paying these deposits now, these payments should be credited back over the first year of the contract.

bins.name: Future revision of hybrid rights
bins.desc: DPN advocates evolution rights allowing hybrid facilities to revise injection/withdrawal allocations as transmission capacity improves.
quotes: Hybrid facilities need to have the ability for future revisions to their injection and / or withdrawal rights as the transmission system capacity increases / improves.

bins.name: Non-firm load classification and study exemptions
bins.desc: DPN proposes non-firm classification exempt from deliverability study while retaining full participation rights.
quotes: However, if a new classification of non-firm loads and backup generation is to be established through this ruling, then these loads should be excluded from the deliverability study requirement as this studies intention is to ensure connection to the grid is firm.

bins.name: Non-firm withdrawal rights value capture
bins.desc: DPN argues non-firm withdrawal rights enable flexible loads to monetize excess load during low-demand periods.
quotes: Additionally, with this new classification of load types, FERC should also require that flexible loads have non-firm withdrawal rights, ensuring that excess load on the grid can be monetized during period of low demand if there is a demand from non-firm data centers to consume additional power.

bins.name: Curtailment commitment incentives
bins.desc: DPN supports expedited study pathways conditional on verifiable curtailment and dispatchability capability.
quotes: Seventh, the interconnection study of large loads that agree to be curtailable and hybrid facilities that agree to be curtailable and dispatchable should be expedited.

bins.name: Proven operator priority in expedited queue
bins.desc: DPN proposes higher priority for operators with demonstrated curtailment track record versus new entrants.
quotes: This reform is essential. There also needs to be an even higher priority for company submissions that have proven their ability to curtail / dispatched at their facilities vs. new entries.

bins.name: Upgrade cost credit over PPA term
bins.desc: DPN supports network upgrade credit-back over PPA lifetime with 50% cap to provide multi-year cost recovery pathways.
quotes: This is already being done, but the upgrade costs need to be credited back to the facility over the course the power purchase agreement (PPA) length. The maximum duration for issuing the credit should be 50% of the PPA length.

bins.name: Non-TO equal build option rights
bins.desc: DPN advocates equal construction rights for non-transmission-owner customers to prevent monopoly delays and cost inflation.
quotes: Ninth, to the extent the interconnection customer is not the transmission owner, the interconnection customer shall be afforded the same (or equivalent) option to build as currently provided to generator interconnection customers.

This prevents the transmission owner from having a monopoly on construction that could delay projects or raise costs. It aligns rights across different types of interconnection customers so everyone has a viable path to get facilities built on time, so long as reliability and standards are met.

bins.name: Transmission service rate allocation by withdrawal rights
bins.desc: DPN supports transmission service charges allocated proportional to withdrawal rights held.
quotes: Eleventh, utilities serving large loads, including those at hybrid facilities, should be responsible for transmission service based on their withdrawal rights, as that value amount reflects the quantity of capacity and energy that is being transmitted across the transmission system to the load.

bins.name: Hybrid ancillary service option
bins.desc: DPN advocates option for hybrid facilities to directly manage ancillary services rather than delegating to utilities.
quotes: Utilities should be responsible and pass through the services with zero fees or mark-ups; however, there should be an option for the hybrid facility to take on the responsibilities for ancillary services.

bins.name: Load submission buffer for operational flexibility
bins.desc: DPN advocates buffer allowance on submitted load levels to enable operational optimization without penalty.
quotes: A buffer percentage to the high side of the submitted load would help too because the facility is paying for the load submitted regardless of whether it is utilized. If the facility cannot operate up to the submitted load, then the hybrid facility incurs additional cost due to not being able to monetize the load by staying under the cap (load submitted).

bins.name: Expedited queue opt-in for existing study participants
bins.desc: DPN proposes existing study participants on track to exceed 60-day completion can opt into expedited track as first in line.
quotes: For large loads already undergoing the study process, if their current process will take more than 60 days, they should be given the option to opt into the expedited interconnection study queue as first in line.

bins.name: NERC flexible load category and market-based standards
bins.desc: DPN advocates NERC establish flexible load registration category and market-based, achievable reliability standards rather than strict compliance mandates.
quotes: NERC should have a flexible load category. Reliability standards should emphasize grid resilience on the supply side and be transparent, achievable, market-based solutions on the demand side.

NERC may impose stricter reliability standards on large loads, especially if flexibility is not adequately demonstrated, increasing compliance burdens. Conversations at these forums are already focusing on how to slow down the interconnection of data centers to match more the speed of infrastructure construction and generation buildout, instead of brainstorming creative ways to speed up these process to the extent they can.

bins.name: NERC and industry forum resistance to speed
bins.desc: DPN identifies conversation in NERC forums focused on slowing data center interconnection rather than accelerating process improvements.
quotes: NERC may impose stricter reliability standards on large loads, especially if flexibility is not adequately demonstrated, increasing compliance burdens. Conversations at these forums are already focusing on how to slow down the interconnection of data centers to match more the speed of infrastructure construction and generation buildout, instead of brainstorming creative ways to speed up these process to the extent they can.

bins.name: State-level tariff risk
bins.desc: DPN cautions states may enact stricter tariffs targeting data centers, complicating deployment.
quotes: States may respond with stricter interconnection requirements or new tariffs targeting data centers and large flexible loads, potentially complicating deployment.

bins.name: Transmission owner accountability for study timeliness
bins.desc: DPN advocates fines or accountability mechanisms for transmission owners and service providers to meet study completion deadlines. Current good-faith model is insufficient.
quotes: Further, there should be fines imposed or some accountability mechanism for TO's and TSPs to complete the studies and their part of the process on time. Delay's caused by TO's is one of the biggest slower of things and could add great gains in time if compliance from them can be ensured by fines etc. At present, they work on good faith and no accountability model.

bins.name: SPP PALS process as operational precedent
bins.desc: DPN references SPP's PALS process as existing proof that non-firm flexible load interconnection pathways are operationally viable.
quotes: There must be a clear pathway to interconnect non-firm, flexible large load- SPP is already considering a process called PALS so examples exists.

bins.name: Bitcoin mining and data center strategic value
bins.desc: DPN argues bitcoin mining and digital infrastructure should be integrated into grid reliability and planning via direct utility partnerships.
quotes: More education around how flexible large load and support the grid and have avenues that allow flexible large load customers like bitcoin miners to work directly with utilities and in the wholesale market. There could be significant financial uplift from reduced O&M spend, reduced transmission development, and uplift on energy revenues by adding BTC mining into the reliability paradigm of the utility and/or independent BA.

bins.name: Direct operator-utility and wholesale market access
bins.desc: DPN advocates education and direct wholesale market participation rights for flexible load operators to capture financial and reliability value.
quotes: More education around how flexible large load and support the grid and have avenues that allow flexible large load customers like bitcoin miners to work directly with utilities and in the wholesale market. There could be significant financial uplift from reduced O&M spend, reduced transmission development, and uplift on energy revenues by adding BTC mining into the reliability paradigm of the utility and/or independent BA.

bins.name: Load profile penalty precedent
bins.desc: DPN cites TVA load profile enforcement with penalty charges as existing precedent for hybrid compliance.
quotes: Hybrid facilities are already required to control their load profile. Typically, any non-compliance will involve penalties. This is what occurs in TVA for loads that are supposed to be strictly off-peak but happen to operate during the peak hours — results in a stiff penalty charge.
